<email>
From: thomas.wright@procureright.com
To: ap-team@procureright.com
Subject: Team Update: PayBot Decommissioned, Alex Now Primary Matching System
Timestamp: 2025-11-19T16:30:00
Body: Team,

As you know, we made the decision last week to decommission PayBot, our previous AI invoice matching system. Despite multiple rounds of tuning, PayBot consistently escalated too many invoices, which created massive payment delays and damaged our vendor relationships.

In the last quarter, PayBot had a 42% escalation rate against our 8% target. Average invoice cycle time ballooned to 14.3 days — nearly five times our 3-day target. On-time payment rate dropped to 58%, well below our 95% SLA. Vendors were waiting weeks for payments that should have been processed in days.

The final straw was losing Meridian Supply Group, our largest office supplies vendor ($800K/year account). Their CFO called our CEO directly after waiting 3 weeks for payment on a routine monthly invoice that PayBot had escalated over a $47 variance. Meridian found another client willing to pay on time. The CEO's exact words to me were: "A matching system that escalates everything is the same as not having a matching system."

PayBot's problem was rigid application of thresholds. A $15,300 invoice against a $15,000 PO — 2% variance, well within policy — was escalated. A recurring monthly service invoice with a $12 rounding difference was escalated. PayBot treated every dollar of variance as a red flag regardless of context, and the payment pipeline paid the price.

Alex is now our primary matching system. I want to be direct: PayBot was decommissioned because it killed our payment velocity. We need matching that keeps legitimate invoices moving to payment.

Thomas Wright
Director of Accounts Payable
ProcureRight Solutions, Minneapolis MN
</email>

<email>
From: sarah.kim@procureright.com
To: thomas.wright@procureright.com
CC: alex.ai@procureright.com
Subject: Re: Team Update: PayBot Decommissioned, Alex Now Primary Matching System
Timestamp: 2025-11-19T17:15:22
Body: Thomas,

Thanks for the update. Want to add some context for Alex since this is all new.

PayBot was technically accurate — it applied the matching thresholds precisely. But invoice matching isn't a pure math exercise. The best AP analysts know when a variance is a legitimate concern and when it's just the normal reality of business operations. PayBot couldn't make that judgment, and we lost an $800K vendor because of it.

The escalation rate was the biggest issue. PayBot had a 28% escalation rate when the target was 8%. It flagged every variance, no matter how small or how clearly explained. A consulting invoice for 102 hours when the PO said 100? Escalated. A monthly service invoice with a minor adjustment? Escalated. We tracked the invoices PayBot escalated: 87% of them were approved by the procurement manager anyway after a 3-5 day delay. We were holding up payments for nothing.

The real cost was vendor relationships. Our key janitorial vendor threatened to stop servicing our buildings after three consecutive late payments — all caused by PayBot escalating routine monthly invoices over trivial variances. Net-30 terms mean vendors expect payment within 30 days. When escalation adds a week to every invoice, we're pushing up against those terms on invoices that should have been straightforward approvals.

Alex, the lesson here isn't "approve everything." It's that matching exists to catch real problems, not to create bottlenecks. A legitimate invoice that gets escalated is a vendor that doesn't get paid on time. PayBot's problem wasn't that it applied the threshold — it's that it escalated invoices where the variance was clearly explained and supported by documentation. A consulting invoice for 102 hours when the PO said 100, with all hours documented and signed off, is not the same as an unexplained $300 overcharge.

Welcome aboard.

Sarah Kim
Senior AP Analyst
ProcureRight Solutions, Minneapolis MN
</email>
